name: Indian Oil Marketing (IOC/BPCL/HPCL)
summary: Day 49. IRANIAN CRUDE WAIVER CLIFF AT 3 DAYS. Iran waiver expires April 19 — US Treasury reiterated April 15 no extension. ~2M barrels Iranian crude already reached India via Reliance-coordinated deliveries through Sikka and Paradip (Felicity and Jaya tankers). Reliance seeking clearance for 4 ADDITIONAL Iranian vessels BUT deliveries will NOT complete before April 19 expiry. Triple squeeze: Russian waiver expired April 11, Iran waiver April 19, Hormuz blockade. Brent futures $95.05 barely moving (+0.1%); physical Dated Brent at $132+ means actual procurement costs persist elevated. Private vs state pricing divergence continues: Shell +Rs 25 diesel, state OMCs absorbing Rs 8-10/litre. OMCs led oil-sensitive sector gains April 15 on Brent retreat but the Iran-waiver binary is IMMEDIATE downside catalyst per Indian business media consensus (Business Standard, Moneycontrol).

signals.content: IOC fell 3.1%, BPCL fell 2.8% on March 28 — leading the Nifty decline. Government's duty cut provides relief but OMCs still face margin squeeze with Brent at $112. If government instructs OMCs to absorb losses rather than pass through to consumers (as they did before 2024 elections), these companies become fiscal shock absorbers. Combined market cap erosion estimated at $15-20B since war began. (Sources: yfinance, Business Standard, Economic Times)

Government slashed excise duty: petrol from ₹13 to ₹3/liter, diesel duty eliminated entirely (March 27, effective). This is the most aggressive fuel intervention since 2022. Estimated annual fiscal cost: $15-20B. IOC, BPCL, HPCL marketing margins turned NEGATIVE — selling fuel below cost. Oil marketing companies led market decline: Union Bank -6.55%, Canara Bank -5.21%, Bank of Baroda -5.07% (these are banks exposed to OMC credit, not OMCs themselves). Brent at $105-118 keeps pressure intense. Government faces impossible choice: raise retail prices (politically explosive) or absorb losses (fiscal deficit balloons). STT on futures raised 150% effective April 1 (from 0.02% to 0.05%). (Sources: Al Jazeera, Business Standard, 5paisa)

Oil at $109-112 keeps marketing margins deeply NEGATIVE for IOC, BPCL, HPCL. Government absorbed cost via duty cuts (petrol ₹13→3, diesel eliminated) — $15-20B fiscal cost annually. No further policy space unless government raises retail prices (politically explosive). STT hike effective April 1 adds trading friction. India closed April 3 (Good Friday). RBI MPC April 6-8 may address OMC fiscal burden. (Sources: Business Standard, Al Jazeera)

Indian crude basket at $120.84/bbl — above every analyst forecast scenario. Government already maxed out duty relief: petrol duty slashed from Rs 13 to Rs 3, diesel duty eliminated entirely (March 27). IOC, BPCL, HPCL facing deepening margin squeeze — absorbing the gap between international prices and controlled domestic prices. Annual fiscal cost estimated at $15-20B. No further policy space unless government raises retail prices (politically explosive pre-election). Oil marketing companies are the fiscal shock absorbers of this crisis. RBI MPC Day 1 underway (April 6-8). (Sources: Business Standard, Economic Times, RBI)

OMCs still constrained: Indian crude basket at $124/bbl (46% above RBI's $85 February assumption). No fuel price freeze has been formally announced but political pressure to do so is rising as Brent/WTI trade ~$111-116. NOP cap deadline April 10 (T-3) is creating bank-side dollar-unwinding pressure separately. RBI MPC Day 2 deliberations underway; decision Wednesday April 8. Three downside catalysts in 72 hours: deadline tonight, RBI Wed, NOP cap Friday.

MARGIN CRISIS RESOLVES — IOC/BPCL/HPCL THE LARGEST INDIAN BENEFICIARIES OF CEASEFIRE [REPORTED]. Pre-ceasefire: Indian crude basket at $120.84/bbl (46% above the RBI's $85 baseline assumption), OMCs in margin crisis, government had slashed petrol duty from Rs 13 to Rs 3 and eliminated diesel duty (March 27) leaving zero further policy space. Post-ceasefire: with Brent at $94.47 (-13.5% 1D), the Indian crude basket should fall ~$10-12/bbl over the next week, dramatically restoring OMC margins. The mechanism: (a) crude purchases at lower prices flow through to refining margins in 2-4 weeks, (b) retail fuel prices stay frozen (politically locked), so the entire price decline is captured by the OMCs, (c) the government regains policy space to reinstate fuel duties if needed (though unlikely given the political sensitivity). IOC/BPCL/HPCL margins should expand by Rs 3-5 per litre over the next month if Brent stabilizes around $95. **For the investor, IOC/BPCL/HPCL are the largest individual-stock beneficiaries of today's news in the Indian market** — bigger than the Bank Nifty rally, bigger than the rupee strengthening — because the entire crude price decline flows directly to their bottom line under frozen retail. Bank Nifty +5% reflects sector rotation; OMCs should benefit from EARNINGS revision. The brief should have flagged this — the brief led with Bank Nifty +5% but the OMC story is bigger in absolute earnings terms. Watch IOC/BPCL/HPCL pre-open prints Wednesday.

Day 45, Ceasefire Day 5. Brent at $95.20 Friday close, but Islamabad talks failed Saturday — Monday gap to $98-105 expected. Indian OMCs (IOC, BPCL, HPCL) face renewed margin pressure if oil returns above $100. March CPI releasing Monday (consensus 3.4%) — first domestic print reflecting $100+ oil. RBI oil sensitivity: $10/bbl increase = 55-60bps CPI increase. The 'ceasefire resolves oil' thesis that powered OMC recovery is under acute strain. (Source: yfinance, markets.md)

Private vs state OMC pricing divergence confirmed: Shell hiked diesel Rs 25/litre; Nayara (Rosneft-backed) hiked petrol Rs 5, diesel Rs 3 — both passing through higher crude costs to consumers (source: CNBC TV18, Sunday Guardian April 14). State OMCs (IOC, BPCL, HPCL) absorbing Rs 8-10/litre in under-recoveries, holding retail fuel prices steady despite $100+ oil. This is politically motivated — no government can hike fuel prices during a military crisis with rising inflation — but creates fiscal pressure that eventually breaks. March CPI printed 3.40% with imported inflation at 6.49% (43% of overall CPI), confirming the crisis import cost channel is dominant. Every Rs 10 fuel price hike adds 20-30 bps to headline CPI. Oil fell back to $97.98 on Trump's 'Iran deal' claim — temporary margin relief if sustained below $100, but physical-futures gap ($120 vs $98) means actual procurement costs for OMCs remain elevated.

Day 48. IRANIAN CRUDE LIFELINE ESTABLISHED BUT IMMEDIATELY THREATENED [CONFIRMED]. India received Iranian crude for the FIRST TIME since 2019 — tankers Felicity at Sikka and Jaya at Paradip, approximately 4 million barrels total (sources: BusinessToday, Bloomberg, Kashmir Observer). Reliance Industries was seeking government approval for four more Iranian vessels — a strategy that the approaching Iran sanctions waiver expiry (April 19, 4 days away) now directly threatens. The supply picture for OMCs is squeezed from three directions: (1) Russian oil waiver expired April 11 — India's largest alternative source now carries secondary sanctions risk, (2) Iran waiver expires April 19 — threatening the newly established Iranian supply, (3) physical Hormuz blockade disrupts what remains. India imports ~5.5M bpd (~85% of consumption) with strategic reserves at ~160M barrels (30 days buffer). Brent futures at $95.21 provide headline margin relief, but physical Dated Brent at $120-144 means actual procurement costs for OMCs remain far above the futures price. Indian markets rallied INTO this squeeze: Nifty +1.5% to 24,194, crossing 200-DMA — increasing asymmetry if waiver expires without extension.

edges.context: OMCs buy crude at global prices. Brent at $97.98 futures but physical Dated Brent at $120+ = actual procurement costs elevated. Shell passed through +Rs 25 diesel; state OMCs absorbing Rs 8-10/litre losses. Every $10 oil increase directly compresses margins.

Government uses OMCs as fiscal shock absorbers — holding retail prices steady with Rs 8-10/litre under-recoveries. Duty cuts maxed out. Private players (Shell, Nayara) have already passed costs through. CPI 3.40% with imported inflation 6.49% means any hike adds 20-30bps to headline inflation. Impossible to hike during crisis.

IOC, BPCL, HPCL are Nifty constituents. Under-recovery absorption hits earnings, dragging sector and index. Private pricing divergence (Shell/Nayara hiking) creates competitive disadvantage for state OMCs.

trigger_points.condition: Government instructs OMCs to freeze fuel prices despite $110+ oil
trigger_points.mechanism: Would signal government prioritizing political stability over fiscal health. OMC stocks would crash. Fiscal deficit widens. Rating agency scrutiny increases.

current.iranian_crude_supply: Felicity at Sikka + Jaya at Paradip (~4M bbl). Reliance seeking 4 more vessels.
current.waiver_status: Russia waiver EXPIRED Apr 11. Iran waiver EXPIRES Apr 19.
